title: 命案目睹记
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\136-命案目睹记-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Claddock questions Joëlleit regarding whether Anna Stravinsky mentioned having a son, but Joëlleit considers the idea unlikely, noting that most girls in the troupe use addresses for pregnancy support and that Anna was not close to the other dancers. Other members of the ballet troupe describe Anna as a habitual liar who fabricated stories about being a mistress to millionaires, a secret resistance worker, or a Hollywood star; one girl suggests Anna actually came from a dull middle-class background. Because the body found in the coffin at Rutherford Manor was too swollen for certain identification, the dancers remain uncertain if it was Anna. However, it is confirmed that Anna decided not to return to France on December 19th, and on December 20th, a woman resembling her took a train to Blakemore, where she was strangled. Claddock considers whether Anna is hiding with a man or if she is connected to Edmund Crakenstall.

In Paris, Klaudia and Deson discuss Martina. Deson intends to search for marriage records between a Lieutenant Edmond of the Fourth Regiment and a French girl named Matilda from the time of the Dunkirk evacuation, though he notes wartime destruction may have erased such files.

At the station, Weatherall reports that no one at 136 Cressida Avenue can vouch for Martina’s identity, as it is a student apartment. Investigations at local hotels show no record of Martina Crikenthorpe or Anna Stramwinsky, though Anna had checked out of the Brooke Green hotel on December 19th. Claddock meets with Wimbush, a lawyer, to discuss Martina’s letter. Wimbush expresses skepticism regarding the claims of a marriage and a son, suggesting the letter is an attempt to extort maintenance from the Crickenshaw family.
